---
title: "Extract audio features from WAV files"
subtitle: "Create a small WAV, extract RMS/VAD, and collapse per-file summaries."
sidebar: cookbook
---

## Use when

You have a directory of short WAV clips and need one matrix per clip plus optional file-level summaries.

```{python}
import tempfile
import wave
from pathlib import Path

import numpy as np

from natural_features.workflows.audio_batch import extract_audio_files

with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmp:
    path = Path(tmp) / "tone.wav"
    sr_hz = 8000
    t = np.arange(sr_hz // 2) / sr_hz
    samples = 0.2 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * 220 * t)
    pcm = np.asarray(np.clip(samples, -1.0, 1.0) * 32767, dtype="<i2")

    with wave.open(str(path), "wb") as wav:
        wav.setnchannels(1)
        wav.setsampwidth(2)
        wav.setframerate(sr_hz)
        wav.writeframes(pcm.tobytes())

    result = extract_audio_files(
        [path],
        resolution_s=0.25,
        selected_features=["rms", "vad"],
        collapse="mean+sd",
    )

    long_table = result.long_dataframe
    collapsed = result.collapsed_dataframe
```

## Inspect frame rows

```{python}
long_table
```

## Inspect collapsed rows

```{python}
collapsed
```

## Why the workflow returns two tables

The long table keeps the time course: each row identifies a file, a time, and
its feature values. The collapsed table deliberately removes time and returns
one summary row per file. Keeping them separate makes it clear whether an
analysis uses within-recording changes or only whole-recording summaries.
Feature names are prefixed with the method that produced them so columns from
different methods do not collide.

## See also

[Audio batch workflows](../concepts/audio-batch-workflows.qmd)
